confidence_rationale: Classic demonstration that sleep restructures recently-acquired representations to support qualitatively new behaviour. Participants who slept after training on a number-reduction task with a hidden abstract rule discovered the rule at more than twice the rate of wake controls. This is a behavioural fingerprint of the schema-revision MECH-275 names: the same input data, after a sleep period, supports an inference the waking analysis did not. Confidence kept moderate because the underlying transformation in Wagner et al. is rule-extraction from a regular task, which is closer to MECH-275's regularity-extraction arm than to the cross-episode counterfactual-aggregation case the claim primarily targets.
failure_signatures: Wagner et al. show insight emerges after sleep, but they do not show that the insight depends on aggregation across many traces -- in principle the insight could come from re-analysis of even a single training session's representation. MECH-275 specifically requires multi-episode aggregation, which this paper does not isolate.

The hidden rule in the number-reduction task is a deterministic invariant of the training data, not a probabilistic structure inferred under uncertainty. MECH-275's Bayesian framing is a stronger claim than Wagner et al. test.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Participants trained on a stimulus-response sequence task with a hidden abstract rule (a number-reduction shortcut) and re-tested 8 hours later showed roughly twice the rate of insight-driven discovery in the post-sleep group as in either the post-wake or the daytime-wake group. Sleep restructures recent memory representations to enable qualitative reorganisation of inference, not just quantitative consolidation.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-275 says sleep-phase Bayesian aggregation produces schema revision -- not just strengthening of what was learned awake, but reorganisation that supports new inferences. Wagner et al. demonstrate the behavioural form of exactly this reorganisation: the same task data, after sleep, supports an inference (the hidden-rule shortcut) that the waking analysis did not produce.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Wagner et al. do not isolate which sleep stage produces the insight effect, do not show that the mechanism is multi-episode aggregation (rather than single-session re-analysis), and do not address the counterfactual-vs-correlational input distinction. The paper supports the proposition that sleep produces qualitative cognitive reorganisation; the more specific claim that this reorganisation is Bayesian aggregation over counterfactual-backed input is not tested.
mapping.source_context: Human behavioural study, n=66 (split across three conditions), Nature 2004. Number-reduction task is a deterministic-rule task; the hidden structure is a logical invariant of the training trials.

confidence_components.notes: High-impact Nature paper, well-replicated. Mapping fidelity moderate-low because the paper demonstrates qualitative reorganisation but not the specific aggregation mechanism MECH-275 names. Transfer risk elevated because the task is deterministic-rule discovery rather than probabilistic schema revision.
